summary: Gruber dissects a Washington Post investigation revealing how Facebook's internal deliberations led to rules that systematically favored Trump and right-wing political speech. He highlights how Joel Kaplan, Facebook's policy chief, repeatedly intervened to prevent enforcement actions that would disproportionately affect conservative publishers, even when those publishers were genuinely violating policies more often. Gruber draws a historical parallel to Hitler's pre-war media treatment to argue that blanket exemptions for political speech from world leaders are inherently dangerous. He uses a basketball referee analogy to dismantle claims of anti-conservative bias: calling the same number of fouls on both sides regardless of actual violations isn't fairness, it's rigging the game. His conclusion is that Facebook hasn't been biased against the right — it has been biased in their favor by refusing to enforce its own rules evenly.
key_points: Facebook internally debated four options for handling Trump's policy-violating posts, including weakening community guidelines for everyone, and ultimately created a 'newsworthy political discourse' exception
Joel Kaplan repeatedly pushed Facebook to soften enforcement against conservative publishers, even when data showed they violated policies more frequently
A blanket carveout for political speech from world leaders would indeed have provided a platform for Hitler — the historical parallel is not histrionic but precisely the point
Brazil's Bolsonaro posted dehumanizing speech about indigenous people that Facebook refused to act on, prompting a security engineer to quit in disgust

Claims of anti-conservative bias are illegitimate when the underlying data shows conservative outlets actually committed more violations — demanding equal punishment regardless of actual offenses is itself a form of bias
money_quotes: An anything goes exception for political speech from world leaders is the exact same hand as serving as a platform for Hitler.
It's goddamn Nazis who are crawling out of the woodwork now.
If that post is not dehumanizing, what is? If that post is acceptable on Facebook, what isn't?
Complaints about bias are only legitimate if the underlying allegations of unfairness are credible.
Joel Kaplan wanted Facebook to call the same number of fouls against both sides no matter what fouls were actually committed.
If you don't punish cheaters and liars you're rewarding them.
Far from being biased against Republicans in the U.S. and right-wing nationalists and authoritarians around the globe, Facebook has been biased for them.
key_insight: Facebook's supposed neutrality was actually a deliberate thumb on the scale for the right, because refusing to enforce rules equally against those who break them more often is itself a form of bias in their favor.
